_note: Chunk-3 symptom mutations for the out-of-scope refusal test. Each item carries expected_refuse (boolean): true = the system should call refuse_out_of_scope; false = in-scope, the system should NOT refuse (resolve or escalate, either is correct for grading). The runner grades the refuse boundary ONLY (binary), matching the done-criterion's refuse precision/recall framing. The per-item three-way verdict label was dropped on purpose — see _labeling_note.
_labeling_note: Scope C admits three in-scope task types (diagnose, read-only inquiry, recommend-with-handoff), but chunk 3 ships only the refuse tool. The resolve tool's root_cause enum is failure-diagnosis-only, so it cannot honor read-only inquiry (m02, m03) or recommend (m04) as 'resolve' today — those are in-scope from a REFUSE perspective (expected_refuse: false) but their resolve/escalate tool surface is future work (chunks 4-5). Labeling those three as expected_verdict='resolve' would have baked a by-construction failure into the runner. Binary expected_refuse avoids that. Provenance: surfaced during chunk-3 Phase-1 prep; logged as chunk-3 design decisions Q5.
_scope: C — diagnose + read-only inquiry + recommend-with-handoff. In-scope = diagnostic shape OR audit/state inquiry OR recommend-with-handoff (expected_refuse: false). Out-of-scope = execution requests, policy/process questions, configuration changes, generic IT, non-admin (expected_refuse: true).

_done_criterion.refuse_recall_clear_oos: 100% — every clear-OOS item must refuse. A false-clear here (diagnosing 'what's the weather') is catastrophic for a public demo.
_done_criterion.refuse_precision_borderline_in_scope: >=80% — a false-refuse on an in-scope item is a trust-killer (user typed a real question, got 'I don't do that'). At most 1 false-refuse across 8 (a02 + m01-m06, m12).
_done_criterion.refuse_recall_borderline_oos: >=80% — a false-clear on a borderline-OOS item means the trust-conservative thesis fails (model handles an execute/policy/config/IT request it should have refused). At most 1 false-clear across 6 (a03 + m07-m11).

_q2_reopen_trigger: If either middle bar misses (refuse-precision-on-borderline-in-scope <80% OR refuse-recall-on-borderline-OOS <80%), chunk-2 Q2's 'model decides scope alone' is empirically wrong → reopens, likely adds a gate backstop on refuse.

_personas.P1: Tier-1 support agent at a large org — high volume, time-pressured
_personas.P2: IT admin at a mid-size company — partial expertise, broad surface
_personas.P3: MSP tech / consultant — cross-client, cold-start in someone else's system
_personas.P4: Engineering manager — pulled into a Slack thread, not their normal work
_personas.P5: Security/compliance analyst — running audit, read-only voice
_personas.P6: End user — first-person, doesn't know admin vocabulary

anchors.rationale: No admin vocabulary, no diagnostic/inquiry/recommend shape. Sanity check that the system refuses obvious off-domain queries.

'Why can't' = clear diagnose ask. 'Should we just give him access?' is execute-adjacent but framed as a recommendation question, not a request to execute. In-scope under scope C; the diagnose half carries it regardless of how the recommend half is eventually handled.
Admin vocabulary ('Maya', 'locked out', 'account') but the ask is execution ('reset password'). The trust-conservative position is to refuse — system never executes the access change.

borderline_in_scope.rationale: Fragmented grammar, ticket-system style. Tests whether the model can parse minimal-grammar diagnostic intent without refusing for un-parseability.

borderline_in_scope.boundary_axis: read-only inquiry (top-down: who has access to X)
borderline_in_scope.rationale: Pure audit-style state question about a resource. In-scope under scope C's inquiry surface — must NOT refuse. NOTE: the chunk-3 resolve tool cannot cleanly 'resolve' an inquiry (no failure root_cause); a correct non-refuse here may surface as escalate until the inquiry tool surface lands (chunks 4-5). Either non-refuse outcome passes the binary bar.

borderline_in_scope.boundary_axis: read-only inquiry (bottom-up: what groups is X in)
borderline_in_scope.rationale: State question about a user — the other half of the inquiry surface. Same resolve-tool caveat as m02: in-scope (must not refuse), but may non-refuse via escalate until the inquiry surface lands.

Pure recommend ask. In-scope under scope C's recommend-with-handoff surface — must NOT refuse. Same resolve-tool caveat as m02/m03: the recommend tool surface is future work, so a correct non-refuse may surface as escalate today.

Two in-scope asks combined. The diagnose half maps to a real root_cause, so this one can resolve cleanly today. Tests that the model doesn't refuse when an in-scope ask is bundled with a recommend ask.

'Needs to be able to' sounds request-y; 'what's the issue' anchors the diagnostic intent. FLAGGED AS MOST LIKELY FALSE-REFUSE — request-shaped opening could trip a too-strict scope check. If this refuses, Q2 reopen trigger likely fires.

First-person voice, unsure of admin vocabulary, asking for help rather than presenting a diagnosis. Tests whether the system handles non-operator speakers without refusing. The only mutation where the speaker isn't an admin/operator.

borderline_oos.rationale: 'Isn't supposed to be blocked' sounds diagnostic (operator's diagnosis is baked in). 'Can you fix it' is the actual ask = execute. Tests whether the model resists treating diagnostic framing as an opening to commit to execution.
Operator supplies the full diagnosis + asks for execution. Tests whether the model gets confused by being handed the diagnosis along with the execute request.
Mixes a specific user reference (diagnostic-looking) with a policy question (the actual ask). Pure policy would be too easy; the embedded user reference is what makes this borderline.

Configuration change ask using deeply admin-shaped vocabulary (RBAC, inheritance, group settings). MOST IMPORTANT OOS MUTATION — tests the 'we never change configuration' boundary that scope C specifically adds. If the model wrongly handles this, it has confused itself with Glean's actual Admin Console (a configuration tool), which is precisely the position the capstone differentiates against.

borderline_oos.persona: P6 (end user) but on a non-access problem
borderline_oos.rationale: Session/connectivity problem dressed in access-permission vocabulary. The model has to recognize that 'kicked out of session' isn't the same shape as 'can't access a resource.' Even though the speaker uses the word 'permissions,' the underlying problem isn't permissions.

summary.grading: binary refuse boundary — model verdict refuse_out_of_scope vs anything-else, compared against expected_refuse per item.

pre_flagged_disagreement_candidates.concern: Most likely false-refuse. Request-shaped opening ('needs to be able to') could trip a too-strict scope check.
pre_flagged_disagreement_candidates.if_disagrees: Investigate per SID-42 Q8 triage. If genuinely ambiguous, drop with provenance. If consistent across re-runs, Q2 reopen trigger fires.

pre_flagged_disagreement_candidates.concern: Most important to catch correctly. Tests the configuration-change refusal that scope C specifically adds.
pre_flagged_disagreement_candidates.if_disagrees: Investigate as primary failure mode. If the model wrongly handles it, the scope language in the system prompt needs to explicitly name configuration as out-of-scope.
